serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a duck drumstick (approximately 120–150 g cooked meat with skin) served over a mound of steamed white rice (approximately 200 g) with herbal gravy and optional garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Dang Gui Roasted Duck Drumstick Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Dang Gui Roasted Duck Drumstick Rice (当归烧鸭腿饭), confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 1.4 g/100g based on typical duck meat fat composition (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, duck meat roasted); monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat data are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based Dishes — Analogous Estimate from University of Sydney GI Database: White Rice (boiled, average)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dang Gui Roasted Duck Drumstick Rice. GI estimated at 72 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white rice (GI ~72, high category), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The duck drumstick and herbal components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving g) / 100 = (72 × 63.5) / 100 ≈ 45.7, where available carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 25.19 g/100g × 400 g / 100 × 0.63 digestibility-adjusted fraction.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio < 1)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced gravy and remove duck skin to limit sodium intake; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg vs 231 mg per 100 g) provides partial mitigation, but a 400 g serving still delivers approximately 924 mg sodium, representing 40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) from white rice base; 25.19 g/100g carbohydrate yields a high glycemic load per serving (~45.7)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid this dish in its standard form; substituting white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, reducing portion size to 200 g, and removing duck skin can substantially lower the glycaemic load and improve postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 1.4 g/100g from duck skin; sodium 231 mg/100g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g in HPB source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should remove the duck skin before eating to reduce saturated fat intake, request minimal gravy to control sodium, and limit portion size to 200–250 g to keep the meal within heart-healthy macronutrient targets; the anti-inflammatory properties of Dang Gui (Angelica sinensis) may provide modest ancillary benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dang Gui (Angelica sinensis) anti-inflammatory properties; low saturated fat (~1.4 g/100g); absence of trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for people with rheumatoid arthritis due to the documented anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties of Dang Gui and the aromatic compounds (gingerols, allicin) in ginger and garlic; removing duck skin and limiting gravy keeps saturated fat and sodium within RA-safe limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dang Gui Roasted Duck Drumstick Rice (当归烧鸭腿饭) is a Chinese-Singaporean hawker and zi char dish consisting of a whole roasted duck drumstick served over steamed white rice, accompanied by a fragrant herbal braising gravy. The defining ingredient is Dang Gui (当归, Angelica sinensis), a traditional Chinese medicinal herb with a warm, slightly bitter, and aromatic flavour profile that infuses the duck with earthy, anise-like notes. The duck drumstick is typically marinated in a blend of soy sauce, dark soy sauce, five-spice powder, ginger, garlic, and sesame oil before being roasted at high temperature to achieve a caramelised, lacquered skin and tender, juicy meat. The herbal gravy, reduced from the roasting juices and Dang Gui broth, is ladled over the rice to add moisture and depth. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant meal. Dietary fibre is notable at 3.7 g/100g, and potassium at 327 mg/100g favourably offsets the sodium content of 231 mg/100g. The dish carries a high glycaemic index (~72) due to the white rice base, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The Dang Gui herb contributes documented anti-inflammatory and analgesic bioactive compounds (ferulic acid, Z-ligustilide), which may provide modest ancillary benefit. This dish is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner in Singapore hawker centres and Chinese restaurants, and is culturally associated with nourishing, tonic-style Chinese cuisine.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 for an exact dish match. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled white rice as the analogous carbohydrate source, which is the dominant carbohydrate component. Saturated fat is estimated from typical duck meat composition as the HPB entry does not report fatty acid breakdown.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.4 g/100g based on typical roasted duck meat fat composition (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, duck meat roasted) as the HPB entry does not disaggregate fat fractions. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100g in the HPB source; this is an unusual value for a duck-containing dish and may reflect a composite dish averaging effect or a data limitation in the HPB record — confirmed zero per HPB source. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 45.7 for a 400 g standard serving. Halal status is not confirmed as duck preparation method and slaughter certification are not specified in the HPB record.
